explanation: The statement requires the system to implement seven distinct operating modes. In LTL, we cannot directly express that the system implements these modes as separate requirements, but we can encode properties about their possible activation. Typically, at any given time, the system should be in exactly one mode. Thus, we write that globally (G), exactly one of the modes is active (i.e., StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | AlarmTestMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode), and no two modes are active simultaneously (&-wise combination). Since AlarmTestMode is not among the atomics, it's omitted. The formula expresses exclusive activation globally.

explanation:
The specification lists seven operating modes (Start-up, Self-test, Standby, Alarm Test, PCV, PSV, Fail-safe). To formalize this in LTL, we generally require the system to always be in at least one of these modes, and possibly that these modes are mutually exclusive (the system is in only one mode at a time). The atomic propositions provided map to these modes as: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, failSafeMode. (Alarm Test Mode does not have a direct atomic proposition, so it is omitted.)

Step-by-step:
1. Identify atomic propositions representing the modes: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, failSafeMode.
2. Formalize that, globally, at least one of these propositions holds:
   G(StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode)
3. (Optional) Mutual exclusion: At all times, no two modes are active simultaneously:
   For example, G(!(StartUpMode & SelfTestMode) & !(StartUpMode & StandbyMode) & ... ), enumerating all pairs.

The minimal requirement, based on the natural language sentence, is (2).
